title: Management Skills Learned 1969–1979
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Peter S. Kastner reflects on the five core professional skills forged during his first decade in computing (1969–1979): defensive professionalism, pragmatic adaptation, fiduciary forensics, high-volume systems logistics, and resilience under chaos. Drawing on engagements at firms including ADL, Chase Manhattan, Houghton Mifflin, and Continental Forest Industries, he argues these 'foxhole' experiences created the analytical and personal temperament that drove his later career as an industry strategist.
importance_rationale: Provides rare first-person testimony of the lived technical and professional conditions of mainframe-era computing in the 1970s, capturing skills and cultural norms not documented in formal literature.
relevance_rationale: The skills taxonomy—forensic accountability, environmental adaptation, fiduciary transparency—remains directly applicable to technology project management and analyst work today.
prescience_rationale: Memoir document; no forward-looking predictions made.
